questions.question: What is the minimum number of weeks of data needed before making decisions based on metrics patterns?
questions.explanation: You need 4 weeks minimum to see patterns. Week 1 might be an outlier. Week 4 confirms a trend. The rule: observe for 3 weeks, then act in week 4.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what increased Jake's follow-through rate from 40% to 85%?

options.text: Texting his 3 action items to his advisor every Friday

questions.explanation: External accountability was the key. Jake texted his 3 action items to his advisor every Friday. Research shows written accountability increases goal achievement by 42%. The act of reporting creates discipline.

questions.question: How many action items should you identify at the end of each weekly review?
questions.explanation: Maximum 3 actions per week. More than 3 means you won't do any of them. Each action must be specific, measurable, and based on data from the review — not vague aspirations.

questions.question: You should only review metrics when things feel bad to avoid wasting time during good weeks.
questions.explanation: False. Reviewing only when things feel bad creates panic-driven decisions. Review every Friday — good weeks and bad weeks. Consistency reveals patterns that panic obscures.

questions.question: Design your weekly metrics review ritual. When will you do it? What 5 parts will you cover? Who will be your accountability partner, and how will you share your action items with them?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response specifies a fixed day and time for the review, covers the 5-part agenda (funnel, velocity, forecast, channel, actions), identifies a specific accountability partner or mechanism, and commits to a minimum 8-week trial period.
